relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Every seventh year debt pressure must loosen its grip.
The creditor's hand is required to release the neighbor.
The ancient law draws an unequal boundary around the foreigner.
The covenant imagines a community where poverty need not exist.
That possibility depends upon practiced listening.
Blessing is pictured as freedom from national indebtedness.
The heart and hand must not close against a poor neighbor.
The open hand responds to the actual lack.
Release may not become an excuse to stop lending before the seventh year.
Giving must not be poisoned by resentment.
Persistent poverty becomes a permanent command to open the hand.
A Hebrew man or woman must be released after six years.
Freedom may not begin with empty hands.
Flock, grain, and wine equip the released person for life.
Israel remembers that redemption from Egypt came before this command.
A servant may choose to remain within a household that has become secure.
The doorway mark makes that service permanent within the ancient system.
The owner is told not to resent releasing six years of labor.
Firstborn animals are set apart from ordinary production.
The household eats them before YHWH year by year.
An animal with a blemish is not placed upon the altar.
It may still become local food shared by clean and unclean.
Its blood returns to earth.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 15 measures freedom by what happens after release. Debt is loosened, the poor person's real need is met, and a former servant receives enough to begin again. Liberation without resources is only another form of abandonment.
